Economics Case Study Series  ·  Unit 5

The 1973 Oil Crisis
& the Birth of Stagflation

October 1973 – March 1974  ·  OPEC Oil Embargo  ·  United States & Western Economies
CRUDE OIL PRICE: $3.00 to $12.00 PER BARREL  ·  INFLATION HITS 11%  ·  GDP GROWTH: NEGATIVE  ·  GAS LINES STRETCH BLOCKS  ·  OPEC CUTS PRODUCTION 5% PER MONTH  ·  NIXON SIGNS EMERGENCY HIGHWAY ENERGY CONSERVATION ACT  ·  HEATING OIL RATIONED  ·  DOW JONES FALLS 45%  · 
Learning Targets: Cartels Price Makers vs. Price Takers Cost-Push Inflation Demand-Pull Inflation Stagflation
Section 01

Setting the Scene: The World Before the Embargo

In the early 1970s, oil was cheap, abundant, and taken for granted. The United States, Western Europe, and Japan had built entire economies, their suburbs, factories, and power grids, on the assumption that petroleum would cost roughly $3 a barrel forever (for context, oil has traded well above $100 per barrel in recent years). Cars were big, commutes were long, and most houses didn't even have insulation in the walls.

Then, in October 1973, everything changed. When the U.S. backed Israel in the Yom Kippur War, the Arab members of the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) responded by cutting off oil sales to the U.S. and several other Western nations. Within months, the price of oil had quadrupled. The result was one of the most dramatic economic disruptions of the 20th century, and it gave the world a word economists hoped they'd never need: stagflation.

"The oil weapon is a political weapon and we are going to use it."
Ahmed Zaki Yamani, Saudi Oil Minister  ·  1973
Oct. 6, 1973

The Yom Kippur War Begins

Egypt and Syria launch a surprise attack on Israel. The U.S. airlifts military supplies to Israel in response.

Oct. 17, 1973

OPEC Announces the Embargo

Arab oil ministers cut production and embargo the U.S., Portugal, Netherlands, and others who supported Israel.

Nov. – Dec. 1973

Gas Lines Appear Across America

Stations run out of fuel. Odd-even rationing begins. Speed limits cut to 55 mph. Sunday driving bans imposed.

Jan. 1974

Oil Hits $12 a Barrel

From $3 in October to $12 by January: a 400% increase in three months. Inflation surges. Growth collapses.

March 1974

Embargo Lifted: Damage Done

The embargo ends, but oil prices stay high. The era of cheap oil is over. The global economy is permanently changed.

The Embargo in Photos
Fig. 1 of 3
A Shell station posts a "Pumps Closed" sign during the embargo, 1973. Stations that ran dry often shut with no notice, leaving drivers stranded mid-commute.
Fig. 2 of 3
Dozens of cars queue at a Standard station in Portland, Oregon, 1973. Wait times of two to three hours became routine. Many Americans rearranged their schedules around the gas line.
Fig. 3 of 3
"Sales Limited to 10 Gals. Per Customer." Rationing signs appeared nationwide. Some states introduced odd-even rules: if your license plate ended in an odd number, you could only buy gas on odd-numbered calendar days.
Section 02

What Is a Cartel? (And Why Producers Love High Prices)

In a normal competitive market, if you raise your price, your customers just buy from someone else. You're a price taker: the market sets the price, and you accept it or lose business. This is how most markets work: wheat farmers, factory workers, small retailers.

But OPEC member nations figured out a way to beat this system. By coordinating with each other, agreeing to all produce less oil at the same time, they could force prices up and nobody could "shop around." This coordination is what makes a cartel.

Price Taker +

A producer who must accept whatever price the market offers. If a wheat farmer tries to charge more than market price, buyers simply buy from another farmer. Individual sellers have no power over price. This describes most producers in competitive markets.

Price Maker +

A producer who has enough market power to set (or heavily influence) price. Monopolies are the extreme case. A cartel creates price-maker power through coordination, acting like one giant monopolist even though many sellers exist.

Cartel +

A group of producers who agree to coordinate output and pricing, essentially colluding to act as one. OPEC members agreed to cut production together so no single member lost market share. The goal: keep prices artificially high and share the profits.

Why Do Producers Love High Prices?
Map: Green = OPEC members  ·  Blue = nations embargoed (Israel's allies)  ·  Red = Israel
Gib Crockett, Washington Star, 1973. Kissinger pinned under the oil embargo barrel.
Why High Prices Work for OPEC
More Revenue Per Unit
Selling less at $12/barrel beats selling more at $3/barrel. OPEC made far more money by cutting supply.
Inelastic Demand
Oil is hard to substitute quickly. Even at 4x the price, demand doesn't drop 75%, so revenue skyrockets.
Political Leverage
High prices gave OPEC geopolitical power: the ability to punish countries that supported Israel.
Section 03

How Oil Prices Drive Inflation

When oil quadrupled in price, it didn't just make driving more expensive. Oil is an input in almost everything: gasoline to move trucks, fuel to heat factories, petrochemicals to make plastics. When the cost of a critical input skyrockets, the cost of making everything rises. This creates a specific kind of inflation economists call cost-push inflation.

Demand-Pull Inflation +

"Too much money chasing too few goods." The economy is overheating: consumers are spending freely, businesses can't keep up with demand, so prices rise. The economy is growing. This is inflation policymakers are used to managing: raise interest rates, cool spending.

Think: Post-WWII boom, tech bubble 1990s, COVID reopening 2021.

Cost-Push Inflation ← 1973 +

A supply shock drives up input costs, and businesses pass those costs to consumers. Output falls (businesses can't afford to produce as much), unemployment rises, AND prices rise simultaneously. This is the dangerous kind.

Think: 1973 oil embargo, 1979 Iran oil shock, COVID supply chain crunch.

U.S. Economic Indicators · 1970–1982
Hover a data point to see context. Orange = oil shock years. Data: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics.
YearOil Price ($/barrel)Inflation RateGDP GrowthUnemployment
1972$3.003.3%+5.3%5.6%
1973$3.00 to $12.006.2% ↑+5.6%4.9%
1974$12.0011.0% ↑↑-0.5% ↓5.6% ↑
1975$12.009.1% ↑-0.2% ↓8.5% ↑↑
Section 04

Stagflation: The Worst of Both Worlds

Before 1973, economists largely believed inflation and unemployment were opposites: if one went up, the other went down. This relationship (called the Phillips Curve) gave policymakers a menu of choices: accept some inflation for lower unemployment, or vice versa.

The oil crisis broke this model completely. Suddenly, the economy had rising prices AND rising unemployment at the same time. The word economists invented for this impossible-seeming combination was stagflation: economic stagnation (slow growth, high unemployment) plus inflation.

Why was this so hard to fight? The usual cures were self-defeating. To fight inflation: raise interest rates, but that makes unemployment worse. To fight unemployment: lower rates, increase spending, but that makes inflation worse. The Federal Reserve was essentially trying to fight a fire with gasoline.

The Policymaker's Dilemma: 2x2 Economic States

Click any quadrant to expand

↓ Inflation + ↓ Unemployment

The Goldilocks Economy. Low prices, people working. Every government's dream.

The Fed's ideal. Prices stable, jobs plentiful, economy growing sustainably.

Example: U.S. mid-1990s: inflation ~2.5%, unemployment fell to 4%. Longest peacetime expansion in U.S. history.

↑ Inflation + ↓ Unemployment

Classic demand-pull overheating. Economy growing too fast: manageable.

Too much money chasing too few goods. A "good problem" compared to the alternatives.

Example: U.S. post-COVID 2021–22. Strong demand drove inflation to 9.1%, but unemployment was low.

Fix: Raise interest rates to cool consumer spending.

↓ Inflation + ↑ Unemployment

Recession with deflation risk. Fix: cut rates, stimulus. Standard Keynesian response.

Economy contracting. People losing jobs, spending falls, prices may drop.

Example: U.S. 2008–09. Unemployment hit 10%, inflation near zero. Fed cut rates and launched stimulus programs.

1974–75 U.S.

↑ Inflation + ↑ Unemployment

STAGFLATION. Cures for one problem worsen the other. No good options.

The nightmare. Raise rates to fight inflation? Unemployment gets worse. Cut rates? Inflation explodes.

Example: U.S. 1974–75: inflation 11%, unemployment 8.5%. Nixon, Ford, and Carter all failed to fix it.

Fix: Volcker (1979) raised rates to 20%, deliberately triggering recession. It worked, but unemployment hit 10.8%.

The U.S. didn't fully escape stagflation until Federal Reserve Chairman Paul Volcker raised interest rates to a shocking 20% in 1980–81, essentially choking the economy on purpose to kill inflation. It worked, but triggered the worst recession since the Great Depression, with unemployment hitting 10.8%.

The lesson: cost-push inflation caused by a supply shock is far more dangerous and harder to treat than ordinary demand-pull inflation. It exposed the limits of the economic tools governments thought they had mastered.

The Phillips Curve
The Phillips Curve predicted a stable trade-off: lower unemployment = higher inflation. The 1973 oil shock broke this assumption entirely, proving that supply shocks create a fundamentally different kind of inflation the curve can't explain.
Glossary

Key Terms Summary

Vocabulary for This Case Study
CartelA group of producers who collude to fix prices or control supply, acting collectively as a price maker.
Price TakerA producer in a competitive market who must accept the prevailing market price with no individual power to influence it.
Price MakerA producer with enough market power to set or significantly influence the price of their good or service.
Demand-Pull InflationInflation caused by excessive demand: consumers have more money and bid up prices. Usually occurs during strong economic growth.
Cost-Push InflationInflation caused by rising input costs (like oil) that force producers to charge more. Can occur even when the economy is weak.
Supply ShockA sudden, unexpected disruption to the supply of a critical commodity, causing rapid price changes throughout the economy.
StagflationA simultaneous combination of economic stagnation (high unemployment, slow/negative growth) and high inflation. Considered nearly impossible before 1973.
Phillips CurveThe pre-1973 economic model suggesting a predictable trade-off between inflation and unemployment. The oil crisis revealed its limitations.
